self_assessment:
**Weakly‑supported items (the source text mentions them only in passing or they play a very minor role)**  

| Item | Why it is weakly supported |
|------|----------------------------|
| **Gaussian kernel SVM** | Appears only once as a brief illustration of a hyper‑parameter (the regularisation penalty C and kernel width σ). It is not a focus of the paper. |
| **regularisation penalty** | Mentioned only in the SVM example; the paper never returns to it. |
| **annealed learning rate** | Discussed only in the definition of the learning‑rate schedule (Eq. 7) and not used as a separate concept elsewhere. |
| **Quasi‑Monte‑Carlo integration** | Appears only in the paragraph that introduces low‑discrepancy point sets; the method itself is not evaluated. |
| **coordinate descent** | Cited as part of the manual‑search procedure (Larochelle et al., 2007) but never elaborated on. |
| **low effective dimension** (as a separate entry) | The same idea is already captured by “effective dimensionality”; the two entries are essentially duplicates. |

---

**Ambiguous items (the extraction could be interpreted in more than one way)**  

| Item | Ambiguity |
|------|-----------|
| **low effective dimension / effective dimensionality** | Both refer to the same property of Ψ; keeping both may be redundant. |
| **low‑discrepancy sequences** | This generic label covers several concrete sequences (Sobol, Halton, Niederreiter). The extraction lists the generic term but does not specify which sequence is meant in each context. |
| **grid‑assisted manual search** | It is described as a hybrid procedure (grid + human tuning). It can be seen either as an algorithmic strategy or simply as a workflow description. |
| **cross‑validation** | Listed both as a “terminology” and as an “algorithm”. In the paper it is a validation technique, not an optimisation algorithm per se. |

---

**Important missing items (clearly present in the document but not extracted)**  

| Missing item | Where it appears & why it matters |
|--------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Halton sequence** | Mentioned together with Sobol and Niederreiter as low‑discrepancy constructions (Section 4). |
| **Niederreiter sequence** | Same paragraph as above; a distinct low‑discrepancy method. |
| **Gaussian kernel** | Described as the “squared‑exponential (or Gaussian) kernel” for the GP model (Section 3). |
| **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** | The acronym appears in the SVM example; a major class of models. |
| **curse of dimensionality** | Used early (Section 2) to explain why grid search scales poorly; a key motivating concept. |
| **high‑throughput methods** | Mentioned in the abstract as a recent class of successful approaches; relevant to the discussion of why many hyper‑parameters can be ignored. |
| **effective dimension** (as a phrase distinct from “effective dimensionality”) | The paper sometimes uses “effective dimension” (e.g., “low effective dimension”) – a term that could be captured separately. |
| **Gaussian kernel** (as a stand‑alone term) | Apart from the SVM example, the kernel is central to the GP modelling of Ψ. |
| **Gaussian process** (without “regression”) | The model is referred to simply as a “Gaussian process” in several places; could be listed as a terminology. |
| **adaptive (sequential) hyper‑parameter optimisation** | The conclusion repeatedly contrasts random search with “adaptive (sequential) hyper‑parameter optimisation algorithms”; the phrase itself is a notable term. |
| **high‑throughput methods** | Already noted above; appears in the abstract and later discussion. |

These observations should help refine the extraction: drop or merge the weak/duplicate entries, resolve the ambiguities, and add the missing but salient terms and algorithmic names.

self_assessment:
**Weakly‑supported items**  
| Item | Why the support is thin or peripheral |
|------|----------------------------------------|
| **Gaussian kernel SVM** (term, line 15) | The paper only uses the SVM example to illustrate a hyper‑parameter vector λ = (C, σ). No further discussion of SVMs or kernels follows, so the term is only a passing illustration. |
| **coordinate descent** (algorithm, line 861) | Mentioned only once in the description of the manual‑grid hybrid search used by Larochelle et al. The paper does not develop or evaluate this algorithm itself. |
| **Bayesian optimization** (algorithm, line 1169) | Appears only in the “future work” paragraph as a possible direction. No concrete experiment or analysis uses it, so its inclusion as an “algorithm” is weak. |
| **learning rate** / **annealed learning rate** (terms, line 135) | The term is present, but the extraction gives a single line number for both. The paper discusses several learning‑rate‑related hyper‑parameters (initial rate, annealing start, etc.) across many sections, making the single‑line reference ambiguous. |
| **L2 regularization** (term, line 140) | The manuscript writes “/lscript 2 regularization penalty” (a LaTeX artefact). The extraction treats it as a clean “L2 regularization” term, but the original wording is noisy and the concept is only mentioned in the context of a specific experiment. |

**Ambiguous items**  
| Item | Ambiguity / possible mis‑mapping |
|------|---------------------------------|
| **low‑discrepancy** (term, line 819) | The phrase appears in the heading “low‑discrepancy sets”, but the extraction treats it as a standalone terminology. It is really an adjective describing Sobol, Halton, etc., not a distinct concept that the authors define independently. |
| **learning rate** (term, line 135) | The paper discusses several learning‑rate‑related quantities (ε₀, t₀, annealed schedule). A single “learning rate” entry does not capture the nuance and the line number points to the equation for εₜ, not the broader discussion. |
| **annealed learning rate** (term, line 135) | Same line as above; the term is introduced in the same equation, but the paper also talks about “learning rate annealing” elsewhere. The single line reference may not cover all occurrences. |
| **cross‑validation** (algorithm, line 18) | The term is used to denote the standard technique for estimating the expectation over Gₓ, but the extraction treats it as an algorithm. It is more of a validation protocol than an algorithm per se. |
| **Gaussian kernel SVM** (term, line 15) | The phrase “Gaussian kernel SVM” is a fragment of a sentence; the paper never returns to SVMs. The extraction may over‑state its relevance. |

**Important missing items (present in the paper but not captured)**  

| Missing item | Reason it should be in the extraction |
|--------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Hyper‑parameter response function Ψ** (or “response surface Ψ”) | Central to the whole analysis (Eq. 3, § 2, § 3). The term appears many times and defines the objective of the search; it is not listed under “terminologies”. |
| **Effective dimensionality** (or “low effective dimensionality”) | The paper repeatedly emphasizes that Ψ has a *low effective dimension* (e.g., § 2.4, § 4, Fig. 7). While “low effective dimension” is present, the broader concept of “effective dimensionality” is a key term that should be captured. |
| **Random experiment efficiency curve** (or “efficiency curve”) | The notion of an “efficiency curve” (Fig. 2, § 2.2) is a core contribution; it is not listed under either “figures” (the figure is captured, but the term itself is not) nor under “algorithms”. |
| **Figure 6** (Neural‑network performance with standard preprocessing) | The manuscript contains a Figure 6 (described in the text and captioned) that shows results when preprocessing is allowed. It is omitted from the “figures” list. |
| **Gaussian kernel** (as a kernel type) | The paper discusses the squared‑exponential kernel for the GP and also mentions the Gaussian kernel SVM. The generic term “Gaussian kernel” is a distinct terminology that is not captured. |
| **Low‑discrepancy sequences** (as a class) | While Sobol, Halton, and Latin hypercube are listed, the umbrella term “low‑discrepancy sequences” (used in § 4) is not captured as a separate terminology. |
| **Quasi‑Monte‑Carlo (QMC)** (as a method) | The phrase “Quasi‑Monte‑Carlo” appears in § 4 and is central to the discussion of Sobol/Halton sequences, but only the abbreviation “Quasi Monte‑Carlo” is listed; the hyphenated form and its role as a method are not clearly represented. |
| **Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD)** (algorithm) | The extraction lists ARD under “algorithms”, but the paper treats ARD as a *method* for analysing Ψ, not an algorithm used for optimisation. It could be more appropriately a “methodology” entry. |
| **Deep Belief Network (DBN) hyper‑parameter space (32‑dimensional)** | The paper emphasizes the 32‑dimensional hyper‑parameter space for DBNs (§ 5). While “Deep Belief Networks” is captured, the specific notion of a “32‑dimensional hyper‑parameter space” is not. |
| **Sequential (adaptive) search** (concept) | The discussion of “non‑adaptive” vs “adaptive” search appears in §§ 5‑7 and is a key conceptual distinction, but no entry reflects it. |

These observations should guide a refinement of the extraction: tighten weak entries, resolve ambiguous mappings, and add the missing but salient concepts/figures without inventing new items.

self_assessment:
**Weakly‑supported items**

| Category | Item | Why the support is thin / questionable |
|----------|------|----------------------------------------|
| Terminology | **low effective dimension** (page 282, line 35) | The paper repeatedly uses the phrase *low effective dimensionality* (e.g., “low effective dimensionality of Ψ”). The exact wording “low effective **dimension**” does not appear; the extraction is based on a close synonym. |
| Terminology | **Monte Carlo integration** (page 295, line 819) | The text discusses *Quasi‑Monte‑Carlo integration* and contrasts it with “true random uniform samples”, but the exact phrase “Monte Carlo integration” is never written. The entry is inferred rather than quoted. |
| Terminology | **sequential model‑based optimization** (page 300, line 1169) | The phrase appears only once in a forward‑looking discussion of future work. It is mentioned as a class of methods, not as a concrete algorithm used in the experiments, so its relevance to the core content is marginal. |
| Terminology | **Bayesian optimization** (page 300, line 1169) | Same situation as above – mentioned only as a possible future direction, not actually employed or evaluated in the paper. |
| Terminology | **coordinate descent** (page 298, line 861) | The term is used to describe part of the manual‑grid search procedure from Larochelle et al., but the paper does not present a stand‑alone coordinate‑descent algorithm; it is merely a component of the expert‑guided workflow. |
| Terminology | **low‑discrepancy sequences** (page 295, line 819) | The phrase appears, but the paper’s main focus is on specific low‑discrepancy constructions (Sobol, Latin‑hypercube). The generic term is only a descriptor, not a distinct concept explored in depth. |
| Terminology | **Quasi‑Monte‑Carlo integration** (page 295, line 819) | Mentioned only in the context of explaining why low‑discrepancy sets are useful; the paper does not perform any integration experiments, so the term is peripheral. |

**Ambiguous items**

| Category | Item | Ambiguity / possible alternative interpretation |
|----------|------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Terminology | **low effective dimension** vs **low effective dimensionality** | The paper’s language varies (“low effective dimensionality”, “effective dimension”). It is unclear whether the authors treat these as identical concepts or subtly different. |
| Terminology | **Monte Carlo integration** | Could be read as the generic random‑sampling baseline (i.e., plain random search) rather than a distinct integration technique; the text never explicitly defines it. |
| Terminology | **sequential model‑based optimization** | The phrase could refer to any number of SMBO frameworks (e.g., SMAC, TPE). The paper does not specify which algorithm, making the entry vague. |
| Terminology | **coordinate descent** | Could refer to the classic optimization method or simply to the “coordinate‑wise” manual tuning described; the extraction does not clarify which meaning is intended. |
| Algorithm | **hyper‑parameter optimization** | Listed as an algorithm, but it is a problem class rather than a concrete method; the entry is ambiguous. |
| Algorithm | **low‑discrepancy sequences** | This is a family of point‑set constructions, not a single algorithm; the extraction treats it as a single algorithmic entry. |

**Important missing items**

| Category | Missing item | Reason it should be present |
|----------|--------------|-----------------------------|
| Figure | **Figure 7** (ARD relevance plot) | The manuscript devotes an entire subsection (starting at line 690) to Figure 7, describing the Automatic Relevance Determination results. Its omission leaves a gap in the visual evidence supporting the claim that only a few hyper‑parameters matter. |
| Terminology | **effective dimensionality** (the exact phrase used throughout) | The paper repeatedly uses the term *effective dimensionality* (e.g., “low effective dimensionality”). This exact phrase is not captured, while a close synonym was added. |
| Terminology | **high‑dimensional search spaces** | The central thesis contrasts *high‑dimensional* versus *low‑dimensional* settings; the term appears many times but is not listed. |
| Terminology | **random experiment efficiency curve** | The paper defines and repeatedly refers to the “random experiment efficiency curve” (Section 2.2). This specific construct is not listed as a terminology. |
| Algorithm | **random search (i.i.d. trials)** – the paper treats random search as the baseline algorithm; while “random search” is listed, the description could emphasize that the trials are *independent and identically distributed* (i.i.d.), a key property discussed in the analysis. |
| Terminology | **hyper‑parameter response surface (Ψ)** | The function Ψ is central to the theoretical framing; the term “hyper‑parameter response surface” is not captured. |
| Terminology | **effective subspace** / **relevant subspace** | The discussion of “relevant subspace” (e.g., “random search has the same efficiency in the relevant subspace…”) is a recurring concept not extracted. |
| Terminology | **low‑discrepancy point sets** (as a noun phrase) | While “low‑discrepancy sequences” is present, the broader phrase “low‑discrepancy point sets” appears in the text and is part of the argument. |
| Terminology | **grid‑assisted manual search** | The hybrid approach used by Larochelle et al. is described repeatedly (e.g., “grid‑assisted manual search”) but is not listed. |

These observations highlight where the extraction is solid, where it leans on inference, where wording could be interpreted in multiple ways, and which salient concepts/figures the current JSON omits. No new items are added; only the gaps and uncertainties are pointed out.
